A: Propose a way to reduce rush-hour bus delays on a single corridor.

B.rule: Treat commutes as packets on a congested network: delay explodes past a capacity threshold.
B.unlocks: Small peak-load shaping (credits, scheduling, batching) that yields outsized latency reduction.
B.justification: In queueing networks, reducing utilization near saturation produces superlinear travel-time gains.

C: Apply the mechanism: Treat commutes as packets on a congested network: delay explodes past a capacity threshold. Small peak-load shaping (credits, scheduling, batching) that yields outsized latency reduction. Specifically for this problem: Issue tradable peak-hour corridor credits to employers and drivers: peak trips spend credits, off-peak earns credits; integrate with payroll/HR scheduling so companies can “batch” commutes into less-saturated windows and get reliable travel-time drops without blanket tolling.

A: Design a fair way to allocate limited meeting rooms across teams.

B.rule: Treat GPU time as bandwidth: jobs are flows, teams are tenants, the cluster is a router with congestion control.
B.unlocks: Credit-based rate limiting, burst borrowing, and QoS floors with transparent incentives.
B.justification: Networking has mature fairness mechanisms; mapping jobs→flows enables predictable, enforceable allocation.

C: Apply the mechanism: Treat GPU time as bandwidth: jobs are flows, teams are tenants, the cluster is a router with congestion control. Credit-based rate limiting, burst borrowing, and QoS floors with transparent incentives. Specifically for this problem: Give each team a weekly credit refill (QoS floor) and let jobs bid credits per GPU-hour; allow limited burst by borrowing future credits and reward early termination with rebates—so the scheduler acts like congestion control, balancing fairness and urgency.

A: How might we teach basic statistics to non-math majors without formulas first?

B.rule: Treat fractions as invariants under reversible transformations: cut/fold/recombine without changing total value.
B.unlocks: Equivalence discovery through legal moves, like a conservation law game.
B.justification: Understanding locks in when kids experience ‘same amount’ surviving shape changes before seeing symbols.

C: Apply the mechanism: Treat fractions as invariants under reversible transformations: cut/fold/recombine without changing total value. Equivalence discovery through legal moves, like a conservation law game. Specifically for this problem: Run a “fraction trading market”: kids get paper wholes, perform allowed transformations (folds/cuts), and can trade only if they can recombine pieces to match a reference whole; after trading, labels (1/2, 2/4) are introduced as names for the invariant.

A: Design a low-cost intervention to improve sleep quality for shift workers.

B.rule: Treat the setup as a thermal RC system with a safety constraint: brick = heat capacity, blanket = thermal resistance, and the control action is when/where to couple or decouple heat flow to keep surface temperature in-range.
B.unlocks: A controllable heat-release schedule (charge/hold/discharge) while explicitly respecting a maximum safe contact temperature.
B.justification: Once you model heat flow and safety limits, you can time and position coupling to shape the temperature curve instead of just ‘adding warmth’.

C: Apply the mechanism: Treat the setup as a thermal RC system with a safety constraint: brick = heat capacity, blanket = thermal resistance, and the control action is when/where to couple or decouple heat flow to keep surface temperature in-range. A controllable heat-release schedule (charge/hold/discharge) while explicitly respecting a maximum safe contact temperature. Specifically for this problem: Sun-heat the brick, then wrap it in multiple blanket folds to create a thick insulating sleeve; place it near (not directly on) the body, and use the thermometer to tune distance and insulation thickness until the skin-side stays 30–35°C. When temperature trends down, briefly increase coupling by moving the brick closer or reducing one fold; when it trends up, increase insulation or distance. This turns the brick+blanket into a tunable heat buffer that avoids burns while sustaining warmth for hours.

B.justification: Behavior spreads when actions are tiny and reinforced, but metrics only improve reliably if rewards are tied to signals that are hard to fake.

A: What is an unexpected way to reduce plastic waste in rivers?

B.rule: Treat plastic leakage as a missing reverse-logistics channel with no profitable backhaul.
B.unlocks: Turn retrieval into a commodity flow by attaching standardized bounties and using return legs of ships.
B.justification: Waste persists when collection is economically negative; paying for sorted returns creates an incentive-compatible loop.

C: Apply the mechanism: Treat plastic leakage as a missing reverse-logistics channel with no profitable backhaul. Turn retrieval into a commodity flow by attaching standardized bounties and using return legs of ships. Specifically for this problem: Create port-side “reverse-vending depots” that pay standardized bounties for sorted plastic; fund via packaging insurance and use fishing boats/cargo ships as backhaul carriers on return trips, making ocean retrieval part of existing logistics rather than a separate cleanup industry.

A: Design a backpack that stays comfortable during long walks in humid climates.

B.rule: Treat smart clothing as a modular interface layer under lifecycle constraints: textile = durable aesthetic shell; electronics = standardized, swappable liner that meets time-to-detach and wash-cycle requirements.
B.unlocks: Upgradeable sensing/actuation without sacrificing washability, comfort, or garment longevity—because failure domains are separated.
B.justification: Most wearables fail when electronics are inseparable from textiles; modular separation matches how garments actually age and get cleaned.

C: Apply the mechanism: Treat smart clothing as a modular interface layer under lifecycle constraints: textile = durable aesthetic shell; electronics = standardized, swappable liner that meets time-to-detach and wash-cycle requirements. Upgradeable sensing/actuation without sacrificing washability, comfort, or garment longevity—because failure domains are separated. Specifically for this problem: Create a ‘core + liner’ system: each garment is a normal-looking outer shell with a single standardized docking seam (snaps + pogo pins) for a thin removable liner that contains sensors, battery, and compute. The liner pops out in under 30 seconds for washing and can be upgraded without replacing the garment, while the shell stays timeless and durable. Keep cost under $20 by sharing the same liner across multiple items and limiting onboard components to a few high-value sensors plus a low-power MCU. It works because the wash/aging constraints apply to fabric, while upgrade constraints apply to electronics—modularity satisfies both.

A: How could we make scientific peer review faster while improving reliability?

B.rule: Treat a paper as a dependency graph of claims; each node has an evidence/test obligation.
B.unlocks: Automated checks on easy nodes, then route only fragile/high-impact nodes to specialists.
B.justification: Review time should concentrate where validity hinges; claim-graphs expose which nodes dominate correctness.

C: Apply the mechanism: Treat a paper as a dependency graph of claims; each node has an evidence/test obligation. Automated checks on easy nodes, then route only fragile/high-impact nodes to specialists. Specifically for this problem: Require a ‘claims table’ and build the claim dependency graph; run automated checks (stats consistency, code/data reproducibility, plagiarism) on eligible nodes, then assign expert reviewers only to the few nodes with high impact and weak evidence, speeding review while raising rigor.

A: Propose a way to help people remember names at networking events.

B.rule: Treat a name as a retrieval key that needs multiple independent cues under time/awkwardness constraints; design a deterministic 10-second encoding routine that produces 3 orthogonal cues once.
B.unlocks: High-recall encoding without tools: phonetic confirmation + semantic hook + contextual binding, each lightweight.
B.justification: Recall improves when any one of several distinct cues can trigger retrieval, and a fixed routine removes social friction.

C: Apply the mechanism: Treat a name as a retrieval key that needs multiple independent cues under time/awkwardness constraints; design a deterministic 10-second encoding routine that produces 3 orthogonal cues once. High-recall encoding without tools: phonetic confirmation + semantic hook + contextual binding, each lightweight. Specifically for this problem: Use a fixed 10-second ‘3-cue handshake’: (1) confirm pronunciation (‘Did I say it right?’), (2) get a one-word hook (‘Is that like the word/meaning X?’), and (3) bind to shared context (‘So you’re here for Y’). Then repeat the full name once while making eye contact. It stays non-awkward because each step is conversationally normal and brief. It works because it creates three independent retrieval cues without requiring notes or a phone.

A: Create a party activity plan for firefighters that feels fresh.

B.rule: Treat sport as visible skill under pressure with legible state, frequent score deltas, and auditability; engineer the game so actions cause immediate, explainable scoring changes and leave verifiable traces.
B.unlocks: Time-boxed phases, drafts, and scoring tied to observable moves plus automated integrity checks—so spectators get momentum and fairness.
B.justification: Non-physical sports succeed when objectives are instantly legible and outcomes track decision quality in real time, with credible anti-cheat.

C: Apply the mechanism: Treat sport as visible skill under pressure with legible state, frequent score deltas, and auditability; engineer the game so actions cause immediate, explainable scoring changes and leave verifiable traces. Time-boxed phases, drafts, and scoring tied to observable moves plus automated integrity checks—so spectators get momentum and fairness. Specifically for this problem: Create a ‘Live Systems League’: teams draft constraints (budget, latency, risk) then solve short optimization rounds where each submission is scored immediately on efficiency/robustness/explainability, producing frequent point swings. The objective is explained as ‘build the best system under changing constraints,’ and the scoreboard updates each round (every 60–90 seconds). Cheating is detectable because submissions must be reproducible in a sandbox with logged traces and automated validators. This works by designing for legibility (simple objective), momentum (frequent deltas), and integrity (auditable execution).

A: Design a backpack that stays comfortable during long walks in humid climates.

B.rule: Treat smart clothing as a modular interface layer under lifecycle constraints: textile = durable aesthetic shell; electronics = standardized, swappable liner that meets time-to-detach and wash-cycle requirements.
B.unlocks: Upgradeable sensing/actuation without sacrificing washability, comfort, or garment longevity—because failure domains are separated.
B.justification: Most wearables fail when electronics are inseparable from textiles; modular separation matches how garments actually age and get cleaned.

C: Apply the mechanism: Treat smart clothing as a modular interface layer under lifecycle constraints: textile = durable aesthetic shell; electronics = standardized, swappable liner that meets time-to-detach and wash-cycle requirements. Upgradeable sensing/actuation without sacrificing washability, comfort, or garment longevity—because failure domains are separated. Specifically for this problem: Create a ‘core + liner’ system: each garment is a normal-looking outer shell with a single standardized docking seam (snaps + pogo pins) for a thin removable liner that contains sensors, battery, and compute. The liner pops out in under 30 seconds for washing and can be upgraded without replacing the garment, while the shell stays timeless and durable. Keep cost under $20 by sharing the same liner across multiple items and limiting onboard components to a few high-value sensors plus a low-power MCU. It works because the wash/aging constraints apply to fabric, while upgrade constraints apply to electronics—modularity satisfies both.
